title.emphasis: Part III: IP Contracting Across Network Topologies

section.para: The structure of the network significantly influences the contractual dynamics and the associated legal risks.
In centralized topologies (Google, Meta, Patreon), the platform acts as the central node, dictating terms through adhesive ToS and exercising control through architecture and algorithms.

listitem.para: Algorithmic Control and the "Algorithmic Editor": Platforms exert control not just through explicit policies but through "soft" control (UX design, gamification) and "hard" control (content moderation). AI moderation systems function as an "Algorithmic Editor," exercising pervasive creative control over user output. This mechanized direction strengthens the argument for an employment relationship (Prong A).

Cascading Misclassification and Joint Liability: Platforms often deputize users as moderators, creating a de facto managerial class. If users are workers, moderators are uncompensated managers, implicating the platform as a joint employer. Furthermore, third-party payment processors, whose Acceptable Use Policies dictate what content can be monetized, exercise indirect control, potentially making them joint employers liable for labor violations.

section.para: P2P and collaborative networks operate on different contractual principles, emphasizing mutual aid and the creation of a digital commons.

listitem.para: The FOSS Ecosystem: The Free and Open Source Software (FOSS) ecosystem relies on sophisticated manipulation of copyright law (Copyleft) to enforce freedom rather than restriction. Voluntary, unpaid contributions are legally structured as gifts or contributions to a community project, governed by licenses (GPL, MIT) and contributor agreements (CLA, DCO). This model operates largely outside the service-for-hire paradigm.
Creative Commons: CC licenses provide standardized frameworks for sharing IP as a public good, allowing creators to contribute their work with minimal administrative burden while retaining ownership.

section.para: Hybrid models attempt to blend the centralized control of platforms with the autonomy of P2P networks.

listitem.para: Open Core and Dual Licensing: Companies offer a "core" FOSS product while selling a proprietary "enterprise" version or offering dual licensing (e.g., GPL for free use, commercial license for proprietary integration). This balances community contribution with a commercial service model.

Cooperatives: Artist or developer cooperatives provide a structure where creators are co-owners of a collective enterprise. This model preserves individual IP rights and creative autonomy while providing shared economic opportunity outside the traditional service model.
